questions.question: You have been assigned to a new project where the workers are behind schedule and the team are not working together well. The team is relatively new and conflicts are arising. Team members are blaming each other for the work not done and some people are not showing up to work on the project at all. What will you do next?
options: A. Meet with the functional manager of that area to agree on team members' time requirements
B. Meet with your team to set a charter, team charter, and define roles and responsibilities
C. Meet with the project sponsor to gain additional funding for resources who can work together
D. Escalate the issue to the steering committee as soon as possible as a project risk
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Creating a collaborative team environment through team agreements, definitions of roles and responsibilities, formal committees with specific objectives, and standing meetings is the appropriate response. The PMBOK Guide 7th Edition (pages 28-29) addresses creating a collaborative team environment. While escalating or requesting additional resources might seem like options, managing conflicts and building team cohesion through clear roles and a team charter is the foundational approach. Simply escalating or replacing resources without first establishing clear expectations and a collaborative environment is premature.

questions.question: You have recently completed a project for a new product and have been asked to manage it after its release to keep it relevant in today's marketplace. Half of your existing team are rolling off to other projects. What will you do next?
options: A. The project budget is nearly depleted, so manage this as business as usual and release continuous features
B. Create a business case and a project charter for your project sponsor to approve
C. Retain as many of your current project staff as possible to continue work on the product
D. Secure funding for a stable team and use current resource research to form a suite of projects to improve the product
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. This scenario describes product management, not project management. Product management involves continuous improvement and maintenance of a product throughout its lifecycle with a stable team releasing features over time. Securing funding for a stable team and creating a suite of projects to continuously improve the product aligns with product management principles. The PMBOK Guide 7th Edition (pages 17-19) addresses product management considerations, which involve the integration of people, data, processes, and business systems to develop or maintain a product throughout its lifecycle. Options B and C treat this as a traditional project, which is incorrect given the context of ongoing product relevance maintenance.

questions.question: You are delivering a critical change and several key stakeholders in your organization have added seemingly unnecessary steps for you to take with extra approvals, reports, and meetings. They are also restricting the people you need from working on your project in a larger capacity. What will you do next?
options: A. Go directly to the people you need to avoid extra back and forth with the stakeholders
B. Raise a risk in the risk register on the lack of access to sufficient resources
C. Communicate more often with the stakeholders and gain a deeper awareness of their ideas and situation
D. Show your stakeholders the resource assignment matrix and project roles and responsibilities
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C. This question relates to the principle of effectively engaging with stakeholders. Stakeholders may add requirements or restrict resources if they are not appropriately engaged. Communicating more often and understanding their ideas through knowledge sharing and regular meetings helps build engagement and awareness. While option D might provide documentation, without engagement and understanding of stakeholder concerns, showing them documents alone will not resolve the underlying issue. The PMBOK Guide 7th Edition (pages 32-33) emphasizes effectively engaging with stakeholders through communication and awareness. Going directly to resources (option A) bypasses stakeholder management and could damage relationships long-term.
